title: Somatic Therapy for PTSD: Evidence-Based Body Approaches
excerpt: Discover how evidence-based somatic therapies help regulate the nervous system and heal PTSD through body-centered methods.
body:
Understanding PTSD Beyond the Mind

Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is often perceived as a disorder of the mind—a jumble of intrusive thoughts, flashbacks, and emotional overwhelm. But decades of research have shown that PTSD is deeply rooted in the body and nervous system. Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, famously stated, "Trauma is stored in the body." This insight has shifted therapeutic approaches toward somatic therapies that recognize the body’s role in trauma and recovery.

Many individuals who’ve tried traditional talk therapy still feel stuck because their trauma is held in their nervous system, outside conscious awareness. Somatic therapy offers a pathway to regulate that system and reclaim agency over body and mind.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy refers to a collection of mind-body healing approaches that focus on the physical sensations and movements associated with trauma, rather than solely on thoughts or emotions. It helps people reconnect with their bodies, notice bodily sensations, and develop new responses to trauma triggers.

Unlike traditional psychotherapy, which often relies on verbal processing, somatic therapy works with the nervous system’s implicit memory. Peter Levine, developer of Somatic Experiencing, explains that trauma occurs when the body’s natural defense responses get stuck—fight, flight, or freeze responses remain locked in the nervous system, causing ongoing dysregulation.

Key Principles of Somatic Therapies

  • Body Awareness: Noticing physical sensations without judgment.
  • Regulation: Techniques to calm hyperarousal or activate the system when numb.
  • Resourcing: Identifying internal or external supports to create feelings of safety.
  • Integration: Bringing body awareness into daily life to prevent retraumatization.

How PTSD Impacts the Nervous System

Dr. Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory offers a scientific framework explaining how trauma disrupts the autonomic nervous system (ANS). The ANS has three branches:

  • Ventral Vagal: Associated with social engagement and safety.
  • Sympathetic: Fight or flight activation.
  • Dorsal Vagal: Shutdown or freeze response.

In PTSD, these systems become dysregulated. The body may be stuck in chronic sympathetic overdrive (hypervigilance, anxiety) or dorsal vagal shutdown (numbness, dissociation). Somatic therapy helps recalibrate these systems by fostering ventral vagal activation—the state of feeling safe and connected.

“Somatic therapy works because it addresses the nervous system directly, where trauma is stored and enacted.” – Peter Levine

Evidence-Based Somatic Approaches for PTSD

Several somatic modalities have strong research support for treating PTSD. Below are some of the most studied and widely used:

Somatic Experiencing (SE)

Developed by Peter Levine, SE focuses on tracking and releasing the physical sensations associated with trauma. SE helps clients complete the defensive responses that were interrupted during the trauma, restoring the nervous system’s natural balance.

Research: Studies show SE reduces PTSD symptoms, anxiety, and depression with lasting effects (Levine, 2010; Payne et al., 2015).

Sensorimotor Psychotherapy

Pat Ogden’s method integrates body awareness with traditional psychotherapy. It emphasizes movement, posture, and breathing patterns that hold trauma, allowing clients to develop new somatic resources.

Research: Sensorimotor Psychotherapy has demonstrated effectiveness in reducing trauma symptoms and improving body awareness (Ogden et al., 2006).

Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR)

While EMDR is often thought of as a cognitive technique, it involves bilateral stimulation (eye movements, taps) that engage the body’s processing systems. Francine Shapiro’s work shows EMDR facilitates adaptive information processing and reduces physiological arousal related to trauma memories.

Research: EMDR is a recommended treatment for PTSD by the American Psychological Association with numerous randomized controlled trials confirming its efficacy (Shapiro, 2018).

Polyvagal-Informed Therapy

Deb Dana translates Porges’s Polyvagal Theory into clinical practice. This approach helps clients recognize their physiological states and use self-regulation strategies to shift toward safety and social engagement.

Research: Polyvagal-informed interventions have been shown to improve autonomic regulation and reduce PTSD symptoms (Dana, 2018; Porges, 2017).

Mindfulness-Based Somatic Practices

Mindfulness meditation and body-based awareness practices help individuals observe sensations, emotions, and thoughts without judgment. This reduces avoidance and builds tolerance for distressing trauma-related sensations.

Research: Mindfulness interventions reduce PTSD severity and improve emotional regulation (Kearney et al., 2013).

Why Somatic Therapy Works When Talk Therapy Alone Falls Short

Many trauma survivors report that traditional therapy focusing on recounting the trauma story doesn’t fully heal their symptoms. That’s because trauma memories are often stored non-verbally in the body. Somatic therapy accesses these implicit memories through sensation and movement, bypassing the need for detailed verbal processing.

Gabor Maté emphasizes that disconnection from bodily signals underlies much of trauma pathology. Somatic therapy restores this connection, allowing the nervous system to complete unfinished survival responses and regulate itself.

Practical Somatic Techniques You Can Try

While professional guidance is recommended, here are some body-based practices that can help regulate your nervous system and reduce PTSD symptoms:

  • Grounding: Feel your feet on the floor, notice contact points, and breathe deeply.
  • Tracking Sensations: Gently scan your body for areas of tension or numbness without judgment.
  • Self-Soothing Touch: Placing a hand on your heart or belly can activate the ventral vagal system.
  • Movement: Slow rocking, stretching, or shaking to discharge nervous system energy.
  • Breath Regulation: Practice slow, diaphragmatic breathing to calm hyperarousal.

Working with a Somatic Therapist

Choosing a therapist trained in somatic modalities ensures skilled guidance through the often intense process of nervous system regulation. They will help you develop resourcing skills, titrate trauma activation to manageable levels, and integrate new somatic experiences.

Look for practitioners certified in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or trauma-informed yoga and bodywork. Always prioritize safety, consent, and pacing in your healing journey.

Conclusion: The Body Holds the Key to PTSD Recovery

PTSD is not just a mental health condition—it is a disorder of the body’s nervous system. Somatic therapy addresses the root of trauma by engaging the body’s innate capacity to regulate and heal. Backed by science and clinical evidence, these approaches offer hope to those who remain stuck after traditional therapy.

As Bessel van der Kolk notes, "The body keeps the score." When we listen to the body and work with its signals, we reclaim power, safety, and presence. Somatic therapy is a vital piece in the complex puzzle of trauma recovery.

If you or someone you know struggles with PTSD symptoms, consider exploring somatic therapy as a complementary or primary approach. Healing starts with the body.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. A. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Payne, P., Levine, P. A., & Crane-Godreau, M. A. (2015). Somatic experiencing: Using interoception and proprioception as core elements of trauma therapy. Frontiers in Psychology, 6, 93.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Porges, S. W. (2017). The Pocket Guide to the Polyvagal Theory: The Transformative Power of Feeling Safe. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Kearney, D. J., Malte, C. A., McManus, C., Martinez, M. E., Felleman, B., & Simpson, T. L. (2013). Loving-kindness meditation for posttraumatic stress disorder: A pilot study. Journal of Traumatic Stress, 26(4), 426-434.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.

faqs.q: What is somatic therapy and how does it differ from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on body sensations and nervous system regulation rather than just verbal processing. It helps release trauma stored in the body, which talk therapy alone may not access.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy be effective for complex PTSD?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapy can be very effective for complex PTSD by addressing chronic nervous system dysregulation and helping clients develop safety and regulation skills over time.

faqs.q: Is it safe to try somatic techniques on my own?
faqs.a: Some gentle somatic practices like grounding and breath regulation can be safely tried alone. However, working with a trained therapist is recommended for deeper trauma work to ensure safety and proper pacing.

faqs.q: How long does somatic therapy take to heal PTSD symptoms?
faqs.a: Healing timelines vary widely based on individual trauma history and nervous system state. Some people see improvements within weeks, while others may need months or longer for substantial recovery.

affiliateProducts.description: Essential reading that explains how trauma impacts the body and nervous system, foundational for understanding somatic healing.

affiliateProducts.title: In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: A clear guide to Somatic Experiencing and how the body can release trauma through natural defense responses.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Practical applications of Polyvagal Theory for therapists and individuals seeking nervous system regulation.

affiliateProducts.title: Trauma and the Body by Pat Ogden
affiliateProducts.description: Explores Sensorimotor Psychotherapy techniques integrating body awareness with trauma treatment.

metaTitle: Somatic Therapy for PTSD: Body-Based Healing Approaches
